Sadly, even today, people still try to create false gods in their hearts, sometimes in different ways.

17.o: No creature which had any blemish was to be offered in sacrifice to God. We are thus called to remember the perfect, pure, and spotless sacrifice of Christ, and reminded to serve God with the best of our abilities, time, and possession, or our pretended obedience will be hateful to him. So great a punishment as death, so remarkable a death as stoning, must be inflicted on the Jewish idolater. Let all who in our day set up idols in their hearts, remember how God punished this crime in Israel.
17.m:
No animal with any flaw was allowed to be offered as a sacrifice to God. This reminds us to think about Jesus' perfect, clean sacrifice. It tells us to give God our very best efforts, time, and possessions. Otherwise, our pretend obedience would be offensive to Him.

The punishment for worshiping idols among the Jews was severe—death by stoning. Today, those who have idols in their hearts should remember how seriously God took this sin back then.

17.o: Courts of judgment were to be set up in every city. Though their judgment had not the Divine authority of an oracle, it was the judgment of wise, prudent, experienced men, and had the advantage of a Divine promise.
17.m: Courts of justice were to be established in every city. Even though their decisions did not have the divine authority of a prophecy, they came from wise, experienced people. And these courts had the benefit of a promise from God.

17.o: God himself was in a particular manner Israel's King; and if they set another over them, it was necessary that he should choose the person. Accordingly, when the people desired a king, they applied to Samuel, a prophet of the Lord. In all cases, God's choice, if we can but know it, should direct, determine, and overrule ours. Laws are given for the prince that should be elected. He must carefully avoid every thing that would turn him from God and religion. Riches, honours, and pleasures, are three great hinderances of godliness, (the lusts of the flesh, the lusts of the eye, and the pride of life,) especially to those in high stations; against these the king is here warned. The king must carefully study the law of God, and make that his rule; and having a copy of the Scriptures of his own writing, must read therein all the days of his life. It is not enough to have Bibles, but we must use them, use them daily, as long as we live. Christ's scholars never learn above their Bibles, but will have constant occasion for them, till they come to that world where knowledge and love will be made perfect. The king's writing and reading were as nothing, if he did not practise what he wrote and read. And those who fear God and keep his commandments, will fare the better for it even in this world.
17.m:
God was Israel's king in a special way. When they wanted another king, they asked Samuel, a prophet chosen by God. 

When the people asked for a king, they talked to Samuel, who was picked by God. 

The laws for the future king were given. He had to stay away from things that could pull him away from God and religion. Wealth, honor, and pleasure often get in the way of being close to God, especially for leaders. These dangers are pointed out to the king.

The king needed to study God’s law and follow it. He should write down the Scriptures and read them every day for his whole life. Having Bibles isn’t enough—we need to read them regularly too.

Christ’s followers always need their Bibles. They’ll keep using them until they reach heaven, where understanding and love will be complete.

It wasn't enough for the king just to write and read the law—he had to put it into practice. People who respect God and obey his commands do better in this world.

18.o: Care is taken that the priests entangle not themselves with the affairs of this life, nor enrich themselves with the wealth of this world; they have better things to mind. Care is likewise taken that they want not the comforts and conveniences of this life. The people must provide for them. He that has the benefit of solemn religious assemblies, ought to give help for the comfortable support of those that minister in such assemblies.
18.m:
The rules ensure that priests do not get caught up in worldly matters or become rich from this world’s wealth. They have more important things to focus on. 

There is also care to make sure they have what they need to live comfortably. The people should provide for them. Someone who benefits from religious services should help support those who lead these services.

18.o: Was it possible that a people so blessed with Divine institutions, should ever be in any danger of making those their teachers whom God had made their captives? They were in danger; therefore, after many like cautions, they are charged not to do after the abominations of the nations of Canaan. All reckoning of lucky or unlucky days, all charms for diseases, all amulets or spells to prevent evil, fortune-telling, &c. are here forbidden. These are so wicked as to be a chief cause of the rooting out of the Canaanites. It is amazing to think that there should be any pretenders of this kind in such a land, and day of light, as we live in. They are mere impostors who blind and cheat their followers.
18.m:
Could a people with such blessings from God really risk following the lead of those who were once their captives? Yes, they could. So, after many warnings, they were told not to copy the bad practices of the Canaanite nations.

All kinds of superstitious customs are banned here—like thinking some days are luckier than others, using magic charms for illnesses, wearing amulets to avoid harm, or seeking fortunes. Such practices are so wrong that they led to the destruction of the Canaanites. 

It’s surprising that anyone would still pretend to have these powers in our enlightened times. These charlatans only trick and confuse their followers.

18.o: It is here promised concerning Christ, that there should come a Prophet, great above all the prophets; by whom God would make known himself and his will to the children of men, more fully and clearly than he had ever done before. He is the Light of the world, Joh 8:12. He is the World by whom God speaks to us, Joh 1:1; Heb 1:2. In his birth he should be one of their nation. In his resurrection he should be raised up at Jerusalem, and from thence his doctrine should go forth to all the world. Thus God, having raised up his Son Christ Jesus, sent him to bless us. He should be like unto Moses, only above him. This prophet is come, even JESUS; and is “He that should come,” and we are to look for no other. The view of God which he gives, will not terrify or overwhelm, but encourages us. He speaks with fatherly affection and Divine authority united. Whoever refuses to listen to Jesus Christ, shall find it is at his peril; the same that is the Prophet is to be his Judge, Joh 12:48. Woe then to those who refuse to hearken to His voice, to accept His salvation, or yield obedience to His sway! But happy they who trust in Him, and obey Him. He will lead them in the paths of safety and peace, until He brings them to the land of perfect light, purity, and happiness. Here is a caution against false prophets. It highly concerns us to have a right touchstone wherewith to try the word we hear, that we may know what that word is which the Lord has not spoken. Whatever is against the plain sense of the written word, or which gives countenance or encouragement to sin, we may be sure is not that which the Lord has spoken.
18.m:
Here is promised about Christ that a great Prophet would come, greater than all others. By this Prophet, God would show Himself and His will to people more fully and clearly than ever before. 

This Prophet is the Light of the world, John 8:12. He is the Word through whom God speaks to us, John 1:1; Hebrews 1:2. At birth, He would be part of their nation. After rising from the dead, He would rise in Jerusalem, and His teachings would spread across the whole world. So God raised His Son, Jesus, and sent Him to bring blessings to us. 

Jesus would be like Moses, yet greater. When this Prophet came, it was Jesus. And He is "the One who should come," so we do not need to wait for anyone else. The way God shows Himself through Jesus comforts us. Jesus speaks with both fatherly love and divine authority. Anyone who ignores Jesus will face trouble because the same person who is the Prophet will judge them, John 12:48. 

Woe to those who refuse to listen to Jesus, accept His salvation, or follow His commands! But blessed are those who trust in Him and obey Him. He guides them safely and peacefully until He leads them to a place of perfect light, purity, and joy.

There’s a warning about false prophets. We need a good test to check if what we hear matches God’s true message. Anything that goes against the clear words of the Bible or supports sin is definitely not from God.

19.o: Here is the law settled between the blood of the murdered, and the blood of the murderer; provision is made, that the cities of refuge should be a protection, so that a man should not die for that as a crime, which was not his willing act. In Christ, the Lord our Righteousness, refuge is provided for those who by faith flee unto him. But there is no refuge in Jesus Christ for presumptuous sinners, who go on still in their trespasses. Those who flee to Christ from their sins, shall be safe in him, but not those who expect to be sheltered by him in their sins.
19.m:
Here is the law about protecting someone who accidentally kills another person and runs to a city of refuge. They shouldn’t be punished if they didn’t mean to hurt anyone. 

In Christ, we have a place to run to for safety. But only those who truly turn away from their sins can find safety in him. People who run to Christ will be safe if they leave their sins behind. But those who keep sinning won’t be protected by him.

19.o: Direction is given to fix landmarks in Canaan. It is the will of God that every one should know his own; and that means should be used to hinder the doing and suffering of wrong. This, without doubt, is a moral precept, and still binding. Let every man be content with his own lot, and be just to his neighbours in all things.
19.m:
God tells people to mark their property boundaries in Canaan. He wants everyone to know what belongs to them and take steps to prevent doing or getting wronged by others. 

This rule is meant for everyone to follow even today. We should be happy with our own possessions and treat our neighbors fairly in everything we do.

19.o: Sentence should never be passed upon the testimony of one witness alone. A false witness should suffer the same punishment which he sought to have inflicted upon the person he accused. Nor could any law be more just. Let all Christians not only be cautious in bearing witness in public, but be careful not to join in private slanders; and let all whose consciences accuse them of crime, without delay flee for refuge to the hope set before them in Jesus Christ.
19.m:
A sentence shouldn’t be given based on the word of just one person. Someone who gives false testimony should face the same punishment they tried to give the person they were accusing. This law is very fair.

Christians should be careful about what they say in public. They should also avoid spreading rumors privately. Anyone whose conscience tells them they’ve done wrong should turn to Jesus Christ for help right away.

20.o: In the wars wherein Israel engaged according to the will of God, they might expect the Divine assistance. The Lord was to be their only confidence. In these respects they were types of the Christian's warfare. Those unwilling to fight, must be sent away. The unwillingness might arise from a man's outward condition. God would not be served by men forced against their will. Thy people shall be willing, Ps 110:3. In running the Christian race, and fighting the good fight of faith, we must lay aside all that would make us unwilling. If a man's unwillingness rose from weakness and fear, he had leave to return from the war. The reason here given is, lest his brethren's heart fail as well as his heart. We must take heed that we fear not with the fear of them that are afraid, Isa 8:12.
20.m:
In the battles where Israel fought as God wanted, they could count on help from God. They should trust only in Him. This shows how Christians should fight their spiritual battles too. People who didn’t want to fight could go home. Their unwillingness might come from their situation. God doesn’t want people serving him if they’re forced. 

Psalm 110:3 says God’s people should be willing. When running the Christian race and fighting for faith, we need to get rid of anything that makes us hesitate. If someone was unwilling because they were weak or scared, they could turn back. The reason was so that seeing one person’s fear wouldn’t scare others too. We shouldn’t let our fears match those of people who are already afraid, Isaiah 8:12 tells us.

20.o: The Israelites are here directed about the nations on whom they made war. Let this show God's grace in dealing with sinners. He proclaims peace, and beseeches them to be reconciled. Let it also show us our duty in dealing with our brethren. Whoever are for war, we must be for peace. Of the cities given to Israel, none of their inhabitants must be left. Since it could not be expected that they should be cured of their idolatry, they would hurt Israel. These regulations are not the rules of our conduct, but Christ's law of love. The horrors of war must fill the feeling heart with anguish upon every recollection; and are proofs of the wickedness of man, the power of Satan, and the just vengeance of God, who thus scourges a guilty world. But how dreadful their case who are engaged in unequal conflict with their Maker, who will not submit to render him the easy tribute of worship and praise! Certain ruin awaits them. Let neither the number nor the power of the enemies of our souls dismay us; nor let even our own weakness cause us to tremble or to faint. The Lord will save us; but in this war let none engage whose hearts are fond of the world, or afraid of the cross and the conflict. Care is here taken that in besieging cities the fruit-trees should not be destroyed. God is a better friend to man than he is to himself; and God's law consults our interests and comforts; while our own appetites and passions, which we indulge, are enemies to our welfare. Many of the Divine precepts restrain us from destroying that which is for our life and food. The Jews understand this as forbidding all wilful waste upon any account whatsoever. Every creature of God is good; as nothing is to be refused, so nothing is to be abused. We may live to want what we carelessly waste.
20.m:
Here are the Israelites told what to do when fighting other nations. This shows God’s kindness toward people who have done wrong. God asks them to make peace and become friends. It also teaches us how to treat others kindly. 

When there is war, we should always try to make peace instead. In the cities given to Israel, none of the people living there were allowed to stay because they might keep worshipping idols, which would harm the Israelites. 

These rules don’t apply to Christians today, but Jesus taught us to love one another. War causes great sadness and shows how evil humans can be, how powerful Satan is, and how God punishes those who do wrong. People who fight against God will surely face destruction.

Don’t be scared by the size or strength of anything that opposes you spiritually. Trust that God will protect you. But only those willing to give up worldly desires and take up the hard work of following Jesus should join this battle.

God cares more about us than we do ourselves. His laws help us live well and enjoy life. Sometimes, God tells us not to destroy things that could feed or help us. For example, during sieges, fruit trees shouldn't be chopped down. 

Jews think this rule means we shouldn’t waste anything at all. Everything God created is good, so use it wisely without wasting it. You might need what you throw away someday.

21.o: If a murderer could not be found out, great solemnity is provided for putting away the guilt from the land, as an expression of dread and detesting of that sin. The providence of God has often wonderfully brought to light these hidden works of darkness, and the sin of the guilty has often strangely found them out. The dread of murder should be deeply impressed upon every heart, and all should join in detecting and punishing those who are guilty. The elders were to profess that they had not been any way aiding or abetting the sin. The priests were to pray to God for the country and nation, that God would be merciful. We must empty that measure by our prayers, which others are filling by their sins. All would be taught by this solemnity, to use the utmost care and diligence to prevent, discover, and punish murder. We may all learn from hence to take heed of partaking in other men's sins. And we have fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness, if we do not reprove them.
21.m:
If someone was murdered but the killer couldn’t be found, there was a special ceremony to remove the guilt from the land. This showed how much people feared and hated murder. 

God often reveals hidden crimes in surprising ways. Sinful acts often come back to hurt the guilty person. Everyone should work hard to find murderers and punish them. 

The leaders promised they hadn't helped hide the crime. Priests prayed to God asking him to forgive the country. Our prayers can help fix the wrongs caused by others’ sins. 

This ceremony taught everyone to be very careful about stopping, finding, and punishing murders. It teaches us today to avoid being part of other people’s sins. If we don’t speak up against bad deeds, we share in them.

21.o: By this law a soldier was allowed to marry his captive, if he pleased. This might take place upon some occasions; but the law does not show any approval of it. It also intimates how binding the laws of justice and honour are in marriage; which is a sacred engagement.
21.m: A soldier could marry a woman he captured if he wanted to. The law allows for this to happen sometimes, but it doesn't mean it's good. It also shows how important rules of fairness and honor are in marriage, which is a serious promise.

21.o: This law restrains men from disinheriting their eldest sons without just cause. The principle in this case as to children, is still binding to parents; they must give children their right without partiality.
21.m: This rule stops people from taking away an inheritance from their oldest son unless there is a good reason. When it comes to children, parents should still follow this rule. They need to give each child what is rightfully theirs without being unfair.

21.o: Observe how the criminal is here described. He is a stubborn and rebellious son. No child was to fare the worse for weakness of capacity, slowness, or dulness, but for wilfulness and obstinacy. Nothing draws men into all manner of wickedness, and hardens them in it more certainly and fatally, than drunkenness. When men take to drinking, they forget the law of honouring parents. His own father and mother must complain of him to the elders of the city. Children who forget their duty, must thank themselves, and not blame their parents, if they are regarded with less and less affection. He must be publicly stoned to death by the men of his city. Disobedience to a parent's authority must be very evil, when such a punishment was ordered; nor is it less provoking to God now, though it escapes punishment in this world. But when young people early become slaves to sensual appetites, the heart soon grows hard, and the conscience callous; and we can expect nothing but rebellion and destruction.
21.m:
Notice how the criminal is described here. He is a stubborn and rebellious son. A child wasn’t punished because of being slow or not smart, but because he was willful and obstinate. Drunkenness leads people into all kinds of wickedness and makes them harder to change. 

When someone starts drinking, they often forget about honoring their parents. The son’s own father and mother had to tell the city leaders about him. Children who don’t do their duties should blame themselves, not their parents, for losing love and respect. 

He would be publicly stoned to death by the men of the city. Such harsh punishment shows how seriously God views disobedience toward parents. Even today, disobeying parents still angers God, even if there isn’t earthly punishment. 

Young people who let themselves get controlled by desires quickly become hardened and insensitive. We shouldn’t be surprised when they turn to rebellion and ruin.

21.o: By the law of Moses, the touch of a dead body was defiling, therefore dead bodies must not be left hanging, as that would defile the land. There is one reason here which has reference to Christ; “He that is hanged is accursed of God;” that is, it is the highest degree of disgrace and reproach. Those who see a man thus hanging between heaven and earth, will conclude him abandoned of both, and unworthy of either. Moses, by the Spirit, uses this phrase of being accursed of God, when he means no more than being treated most disgracefully, that it might afterward be applied to the death of Christ, and might show that in it he underwent the curse of the law for us; which proves his love, and encourages to faith in him.
21.m:
Under Jewish law, touching a dead body made you unclean. So dead bodies couldn’t stay hanging up because that would make the land unclean too. 

There’s another reason here about Jesus. The person who hangs on a cross is cursed by God. That means it’s the worst kind of shame. When people see someone hanging there, they think that person is rejected by both heaven and earth.

Moses used this word "cursed" to describe the shame of hanging. He did this so later people could apply it to Jesus’ death. This shows that Jesus took the punishment for our sins, proving his love and encouraging us to believe in him.

22.o: If we duly regard the golden rule of “doing to others as we would they should do unto us,” many particular precepts might be omitted. We can have no property in any thing that we find. Religion teaches us to be neighbourly, and to be ready to do all good offices to all men. We know not how soon we may have occasion for help.
22.m: If we follow the rule of treating others how we want to be treated, we don’t need many other rules. We cannot claim ownership over anything we find. Religion tells us to be kind to our neighbors and help everyone. We never know when we might need someone’s help ourselves.

22.o: God's providence extends itself to the smallest affairs, and his precepts do so, that even in them we may be in the fear of the Lord, as we are under his eye and care. Yet the tendency of these laws, which seem little, is such, that being found among the things of God's law, they are to be accounted great things. If we would prove ourselves to be God's people, we must have respect to his will and to his glory, and not to the vain fashions of the world. Even in putting on our garments, as in eating or in drinking, all must be done with a serious regard to preserve our own and others' purity in heart and actions. Our eye should be single, our heart simple, and our behaviour all of a piece.
22.m: God takes care of even the smallest details in our lives. His rules apply to everything, so we should always follow them out of respect for him. These small rules are important because they come from God’s law. To show we belong to God, we need to focus on what he wants and honor him, not just follow the world’s styles. Whether we’re getting dressed, eating, or drinking, we should do it carefully to keep our hearts and actions pure. We should have one goal and act consistently.

22.o: These and the like regulations might be needful then, and yet it is not necessary that we should curiously examine respecting them. The laws relate to the seventh commandment, laying a restraint upon fleshly lusts which war against the soul.
22.m: These rules might have been needed back then, but we don't need to look at them closely now. The laws help us follow the seventh commandment by keeping our desires under control.

23.o: We ought to value the privileges of God's people, both for ourselves and for our children, above all other advantages. No personal blemishes, no crimes of our forefathers, no difference of nation, shuts us out under the Christian dispensation. But an unsound heart will deprive us of blessings; and a bad example, or an unsuitable marriage, may shut our children from them.
23.m: We should treasure the benefits of being part of God’s people, both for ourselves and for our kids, more than any other advantage. Personal flaws, past mistakes by our ancestors, or differences in nationality do not stop us from joining Christianity. However, if we have a bad attitude or set a poor example, or if we marry someone who isn’t right, it could keep our children from these blessings.

23.o: The camp of the Lord must have nothing offensive in it. If there must be this care taken to preserve the body clean, much more should we be careful to keep the mind pure.
23.m: The camp of the Lord should have nothing unpleasant in it. Since we need to take care to keep our bodies clean, we should be even more careful to keep our minds pure.

23.o: It is honourable to shelter and protect the weak, provided they are not wicked. Proselytes and converts to the truth, should be treated with particular tenderness, that they may have no temptation to return to the world. We cannot honour God with our substance, unless it be honestly and honourably come by. It must not only be considered what we give, but how we got it. Where the borrower gets, or hopes to get, it is just that the lender should share the gain; but to him that borrows for necessary food, pity must be showed. That which is gone out of thy lips, as a solemn and deliberate vow, must not be recalled, but thou shalt keep and perform it punctually and fully. They were allowed to pluck and eat of the corn or grapes that grew by the road side; only they must not carry any away. This law intimated what great plenty of corn and wine they should have in Canaan. It provided for the support of poor travellers, and teaches us to be kind to such, teaches us to be ready to distribute, and not to think every thing lost that is given away. Yet it forbids us to abuse the kindness of friends, or to take advantage of what is allowed. Faithfulness to their engagements should mark the people of God; and they should never encroach upon others.
23.m:
It’s honorable to help those who are weak, especially if they aren’t bad people. New believers should be treated gently so they won’t want to go back to their old ways. You can’t honor God with money unless you earned it fairly. What matters isn't just what you give, but how you got it.

If someone lends money and helps the person earn more, both should share the profit. But if someone needs food because they’re hungry, show them mercy. 

Once you’ve made a promise, keep it. Don’t break your word. People could pick fruit from fields near roads to eat while traveling. They weren’t allowed to take anything home though. This rule meant there would be lots of food in Canaan. It helped travelers too, showing them kindness.

But don’t misuse other people’s generosity. Be honest and fair. Those who follow God should always keep their promises. And they shouldn’t take more than they need.

24.o: Where the providence of God, or his own wrong choice in marriage, has allotted to a Christian a trial instead of a help meet; he will from his heart prefer bearing the cross, to such relief as tends to sin, confusion, and misery. Divine grace will sanctify this cross, support under it, and teach so to behave, as will gradually render it more tolerable.
24.m: When God's plan or a person's poor choices put a Christian through a hard time instead of giving them a good partner, they should choose to bear their troubles rather than find relief that leads to sin, confusion, and unhappiness. God's grace will make the struggle better, give strength to handle it, and show how to act in ways that slowly make things easier over time.

24.o: It is of great consequence that love be kept up between husband and wife; that they carefully avoid every thing which might make them strange one to another. Man-stealing was a capital crime, which could not be settled, as other thefts, by restitution. The laws concerning leprosy must be carefully observed. Thus all who feel their consciences under guilt and wrath, must not cover it, or endeavour to shake off their convictions; but by repentance, and prayer, and humble confession, take the way to peace and pardon. Some orders are given about pledges for money lent. This teaches us to consult the comfort and subsistence of others, as much as our own advantage. Let the poor debtor sleep in his own raiment, and praise God for thy kindness to him. Poor debtors ought to feel more than commonly they do, the goodness of creditors who do not take all the advantage of the law against them, nor should this ever be looked upon as weakness.
24.m:
It’s very important for husbands and wives to stay loving toward each other and to avoid anything that would make them distant from each other. Kidnapping was a serious crime that couldn’t be fixed by just paying back what was stolen. People had to follow the rules about leprosy closely. 

Those who know they have done wrong must not hide it or try to ignore their feelings of guilt. Instead, they need to repent, pray, and confess humbly so they can find peace and forgiveness.

There were instructions about keeping items safe until debts were paid. This shows we should care about others’ well-being as much as our own. When a poor person sleeps in their own clothes because you let them keep their things, thank God for being kind to them. Debtors should appreciate more the mercy shown by those who don’t take full advantage of the law against them, and creditors shouldn’t see this kindness as a sign of weakness.

24.o: It is not hard to prove that purity, piety, justice, mercy, fair conduct, kindness to the poor and destitute, consideration for them, and generosity of spirit, are pleasing to God, and becoming in his redeemed people. The difficulty is to attend to them in our daily walk and conversation.
24.m: It is not hard to show that being pure, pious, just, merciful, honest, kind to the poor and those who need help, and generous, are things that please God and fit well with his followers. The challenge is to live this way every day in how we act and talk.

25.o: Every punishment should be with solemnity, that those who see it may be filled with dread, and be warned not to offend in like manner. And though the criminals must be shamed as well as put to pain, for their warning and disgrace, yet care should be taken that they do not appear totally vile. Happy those who are chastened of the Lord to humble them, that they should not be condemned with the world to destruction.
25.m: Every punishment should be done seriously so that people who watch will feel afraid and learn not to do the same wrong things. The guilty ones need to be shown shame along with feeling pain, to warn others and humiliate them. But we shouldn’t show them as completely bad. Those who are corrected by the Lord to help them become humble are truly blessed, so they won’t end up being destroyed with everyone else.

25.o: This is a charge to husbandmen. It teaches us to make much of the animals that serve us. But we must learn, not only to be just, but kind to all who are employed for the good of our better part, our souls, 1Co 9:9.
25.m: This passage gives instructions to farmers. It tells us to take care of the animals that help us. We should also treat kindly everyone who works for our benefit, especially those helping our spiritual lives, as mentioned in 1 Corinthians 9:9.

25.o: The custom here regulated seems to have been in the Jewish law in order to keep inheritances distinct; now it is unlawful.
25.m: This rule here was part of the Jewish law to keep properties separate. Now it is not allowed.

25.o: Dishonest gain always brings a curse on men's property, families, and souls. Happy those who judge themselves, repent of and forsake their sins, and put away evil things, that they may not be condemned of the Lord.
25.m: Unfair money brings curses on people's possessions, families, and souls. Those who are happy find their way by judging themselves, feeling sorry for their wrongs, and getting rid of bad things so they won't be punished by the Lord.

25.o: Let every persecutor and injurer of God's people take warning from the case of the Amalekites. The longer it is before judgement comes, the more dreadful will it be at last. Amalek may remind us of the foes of our souls. May we be enabled to slay all our lusts, all the corruptions both within and without, all the powers of darkness and of the world, which oppose our way to the blessed Saviour.
25.m:
Every person who hurts God’s people should learn from what happened to the Amalekites. The longer God waits to punish them, the worse their punishment will be. 

Amalek can remind us of the enemies of our souls. Let us be able to defeat all our bad desires, all the evil inside and outside us, and all the forces that try to stop us from reaching the Savior.

26.o: When God has made good his promises to us, he expects we should own it to the honour of his faithfulness. And our creature comforts are doubly sweet, when we see them flowing from the fountain of the promise. The person who offered his first-fruits, must remember and own the mean origin of that nation, of which he was a member. A Syrian ready to perish was my father. Jacob is here called a Syrian. Their nation in its infancy sojourned in Egypt as strangers, they served there as slaves. They were a poor, despised, oppressed people in Egypt; and though become rich and great, had no reason to be proud, secure, or forgetful of God. He must thankfully acknowledge God's great goodness to Israel. The comfort we have in our own enjoyments, should lead us to be thankful for our share in public peace and plenty; and with present mercies we should bless the Lord for the former mercies we remember, and the further mercies we expect and hope for. He must offer his basket of first-fruits. Whatever good thing God gives us, it is his will that we make the most comfortable use we can of it, tracing the streams to the Fountain of all consolation.
26.m:
When God keeps his promises to us, he wants us to give him credit for being faithful. Our blessings taste even sweeter when we see them coming from God’s promises.

The one offering the first fruits must remember where their family came from. A Syrian on the verge of death was my ancestor. Here, Jacob is called a Syrian because their ancestors once lived as foreigners in Egypt.

Their nation started small, living as strangers in Egypt and then serving as slaves. They were poor and mistreated in Egypt. Even if they became wealthy later, they shouldn’t get prideful or forget about God.

We should thank God for all the good things he did for Israel. When we enjoy our own blessings, we should also be grateful for the peace and prosperity around us. We should praise God for past kindnesses and future hopes.

He must bring a basket of first fruits. Whatever good gifts God gives us, we should enjoy them fully while remembering that God is the source of all our happiness.

26.o: How should the earth yield its increase, or, if it does, what comfort can we take in it, unless therewith our God gives us his blessing? All this represented the covenant relation between a reconciled God and every true believer, and the privileges and duties belonging to it. We must be watchful, and show that according to the covenant of grace in Christ Jesus, the Lord is our God, and we are his people, waiting in his appointed way for the performance of his gracious promises.
26.m: How can the earth give us its bounty, or what good will it do us unless God blesses it? This shows the special relationship between a forgiving God and each true believer, along with their rights and responsibilities. We need to stay alert and prove that through the agreement of grace in Christ Jesus, the Lord is our God and we are his people, waiting patiently for him to keep his kind promises.

26.o: Moses here enforces the precepts. They are God's laws, therefore thou shalt do them, to that end were they given thee; do them, and dispute them not; do them, and draw not back; do them, not carelessly and hypocritically, but with thy heart and soul, thy whole heart and thy whole soul. We forswear ourselves, and break the most sacred engagement, if, when we have taken the Lord to be our God, we do not make conscience of obeying his commands. We are elected to obedience, 1Pe 1:2; chosen that we should be holy, Eph 1:4; purified a peculiar people, that we might not only do good works, but be zealous in them, Tit 2:14. Holiness is true honour, and the only way to everlasting honour.
26.m:
Moses emphasizes following God’s rules. Since these are God’s laws, you must follow them without questioning. Follow them fully, not just half-heartedly. When you promise to serve God, you must obey him carefully. Breaking God’s commandments after making such promises shows disrespect. 

God chose us to live obedient lives. He picked us to be holy, as in Ephesians 1:4. Jesus made us special so we could do good things eagerly, as mentioned in Titus 2:14. Being holy is truly honorable and leads to eternal glory.

27.o: As soon as they were come into Canaan, they must set up a monument, on which they must write the words of this law. They must set up an altar. The word and prayer must go together. Though they might not, of their own heads, set up any altar besides that at the tabernacle; yet, by the appointment of God, they might, upon special occasion. This altar must be made of unhewn stones, such as they found upon the field. Christ, our Altar, is a stone cut out of the mountain without hands, refused by the builders, as having no form or comeliness, but accepted of God the Father, and made the Head of the corner. In the Old Testament the words of the law are written, with the curse annexed; which would overcome us with horror, if we had not, in the New Testament, an altar erected close by, which gives consolation. Blessed be God, the printed copies of the Scriptures among us, do away the necessity of such methods as were presented to Israel. The end of the gospel ministry is, and the end of preachers ought to be, to make the word of God as plain as possible. Yet, unless the Spirit of God prosper such labours with Divine power, we shall not, even by these means, be made wise unto salvation: for this blessing we should therefore daily and earnestly pray.
27.m:
When they arrived in Canaan, they needed to put up a monument where they would write down the words of the law. They also had to build an altar.

The Word of God and prayer need to go hand in hand. Even though they could not set up other altars on their own except the one at the tabernacle, God allowed them to build one under special circumstances.

This altar was to be made from stones they found in the fields, ones that hadn’t been cut or shaped. Jesus, our true Altar, is like a stone taken from a mountain without human effort. It’s a stone rejected by people because it didn't look important, but God chose it and made it the cornerstone.

In the old covenant, the law included curses that would scare anyone who broke it. But in the new covenant, there is an altar nearby that brings comfort. Thanks to the New Testament, we don’t need those old methods used by the Israelites.

The goal of preaching the gospel is to help people clearly understand God’s Word. Preachers should aim to explain the Bible plainly too. Still, unless the Holy Spirit works through these teachings with divine power, people won’t truly grasp the message and find salvation. We should ask God each day to bless our efforts with His power.

27.o: The six tribes appointed for blessing, were all children of the free women, for to such the promise belongs, Ga 4:31. Levi is here among the rest. Ministers should apply to themselves the blessing and curse they preach to others, and by faith set their own Amen to it. And they must not only allure people to their duty with the promises of a blessing, but awe them with the threatenings of a curse, by declaring that a curse would be upon those who do such things. To each of the curses the people were to say, Amen. It professed their faith, that these, and the like curses, were real declarations of the wrath of God against the ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, not one jot of which shall fall to the ground. It was acknowledging the equity of these curses. Those who do such things deserve to fall, and lie under the curse. Lest those who were guilty of other sins, not here mentioned, should think themselves safe from the curse, the last reaches all. Not only those who do the evil which the law forbids, but those also who omit the good which the law requires. Without the atoning blood of Christ, sinners can neither have communion with a holy God, nor do any thing acceptable to him; his righteous law condemns every one who, at any time, or in any thing, transgresses it. Under its awful curse we remain as transgressors, until the redemption of Christ is applied to our hearts. Wherever the grace of God brings salvation, it teaches the believer to deny ungodliness and wordly lusts, to live soberly, righteously, and godly in this present world, consenting to, and delighting in the words of God's law, after the inward man. In this holy walk, true peace and solid joy are to be found.
27.m:
The six tribes chosen for blessings were all from free women, because the promise belonged to them, as Galatians 4:31 tells us. Levi is included in this list too. Ministers need to take the blessings and curses they preach to others and apply them to themselves. They should use faith to agree with what they teach. 

Ministers shouldn’t just encourage people with promises of blessings. They must also warn about curses by saying that a curse will come on those who do certain wrong things. For each curse, the people had to say “Amen.” This showed they believed the curses were real and came from God’s anger toward sin. By saying “Amen,” they agreed that anyone who did those things deserved the curse.

To ensure everyone understood, even if someone sinned in ways not listed, the final curse covered all sins. It said that anyone who breaks the commandments or fails to follow them deserves the curse. 

Without Jesus’ sacrifice, people cannot connect with God or please Him. The law condemns anyone who ever breaks it, anytime, anywhere. We stay under the law’s curse unless Jesus' work saves us. When God’s grace brings salvation, believers learn to reject sinful behavior and live upright lives. They enjoy inner peace and true happiness by following God’s laws willingly.

28.o: This chapter is a very large exposition of two words, the blessing and the curse. They are real things and have real effects. The blessings are here put before the curses. God is slow to anger, but swift to show mercy. It is his delight to bless. It is better that we should be drawn to what is good by a child-like hope of God's favour, than that we be frightened to it by a slavish fear of his wrath. The blessing is promised, upon condition that they diligently hearken to the voice of God. Let them keep up religion, the form and power of it, in their families and nation, then the providence of God would prosper all their outward concerns.
28.m:
This chapter explains the blessing and the curse in detail. These are real things that have real effects. The blessings come first in the list. God is patient with us but quick to show kindness. He loves to bless people. It’s better for us to do good out of joy from God's love rather than fear of His punishment. 

The promise of the blessing comes if they listen carefully to God’s commands. If they keep their faith alive at home and in their country, God will take care of all their needs.

28.o: If we do not keep God's commandments, we not only come short of the blessing promised, but we lay ourselves under the curse, which includes all misery, as the blessing all happiness. Observe the justice of this curse. It is not a curse causeless, or for some light cause. The extent and power of this curse. Wherever the sinner goes, the curse of God follows; wherever he is, it rests upon him. Whatever he has is under a curse. All his enjoyments are made bitter; he cannot take any true comfort in them, for the wrath of God mixes itself with them. Many judgments are here stated, which would be the fruits of the curse, and with which God would punish the people of the Jews, for their apostacy and disobedience. We may observe the fulfilling of these threatenings in their present state. To complete their misery, it is threatened that by these troubles they should be bereaved of all comfort and hope, and left to utter despair. Those who walk by sight, and not by faith, are in danger of losing reason itself, when every thing about them looks frightful.
28.m:
If we don't follow God's rules, we miss out on the blessings He promises and instead face His curse, which brings all kinds of suffering. Notice that this curse isn’t random—it’s fair because it matches our actions. 

The curse affects everything. Wherever the person goes, the curse follows. Everything they have is cursed too. Their joys turn sour, and they can’t find real peace because God’s anger touches even their good things.

Many punishments are listed here—things that will happen to the Jewish people if they disobey God. These predictions came true during their difficult times.

To make matters worse, the curse takes away all hope and leaves them feeling hopeless and desperate. People who focus only on what they see around them and not on God’s promises might lose their minds when everything seems scary and bad.

28.o: If God inflicts vengeance, what miseries his curse can bring upon mankind, even in this present world! Yet these are but the beginning of sorrows to those under the curse of God. What then will be the misery of that world where their worm dieth not, and their fire is not quenched! Observe what is here said of the wrath of God, which should come and remain upon the Israelites for their sins. It is amazing to think that a people so long the favourites of Heaven, should be so cast off; and yet that a people so scattered in all nations should be kept distinct, and not mixed with others. If they would not serve God with cheerfulness, they should be compelled to serve their enemies. We may justly expect from God, that if we do not fear his fearful name, we shall feel his fearful plagues; for one way or other God will be feared. The destruction threatened is described. They have, indeed, been plucked from off the land, ver. #(63). Not only by the Babylonish captivity, and when Jerusalem was destroyed by the Romans; but afterwards, when they were forbidden to set foot in Jerusalem. They should have no rest; no rest of body, ver. 65, but be continually on the remove, either in hope of gain, or fear of persecution. No rest of the mind, which is much worse. They have been banished from city to city, from country to country; recalled, and banished again. These events, compared with the favour shown to Israel in ancient times, and with the prophecies about them, should not only excite astonishment, but turn unto us for a testimony, assuring us of the truth of Scripture. And when the other prophecies of their conversion to Christ shall come to pass, the whole will be a sign and a wonder to all the nations of the earth, and the forerunner of a general spread of true christianity. The fulfilling of these prophecies upon the Jewish nation, delivered more than three thousand years ago, shows that Moses spake by the Spirit of God; who not only foresees the ruin of sinners, but warns of it, that they may prevent it by a true and timely repentance, or else be left without excuse. And let us be thankful that Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, by being made a curse for us, and bearing in his own person all that punishment which our sins merit, and which we must otherwise have endured for ever. To this Refuge and salvation let sinners flee; therein let believers rejoice, and serve their reconciled God with gladness of heart, for the abundance of his spiritual blessings.
28.m:
If God punishes people, he can cause great suffering in this world. But this is only the start of the troubles for those cursed by God. Imagine the pain in the next world where there is no end to punishment!

Notice what the Bible says about God’s anger coming down on the Israelites because of their sins. It’s surprising that a people once favored by God could be rejected. Even more surprising is that they stayed separate from other peoples despite being scattered across many countries.

If they wouldn’t obey God willingly, they would be forced to serve their enemies. We should expect that if we don’t respect God, we’ll face his punishments. One way or another, God will get our attention.

The Bible describes the destruction that came to the Israelites. They were removed from their land, first during the time of Babylon, then later when Rome destroyed Jerusalem, and again after they were banned from entering Jerusalem.

They had no peace—no physical rest, always moving around hoping to find safety or wealth. Nor did they have mental peace. They moved from city to city, country to country, sometimes allowed back, sometimes kicked out again.

These things show how different life became compared to the good treatment they received in old times. When you compare this with the promises made about them in the past, it makes you wonder. 

When the prophecies about their turning to Christ come true, it will prove the truth of the Bible. This will happen before everyone, showing that Moses spoke by God’s Spirit. He warned sinners about their fate so they might change and avoid punishment.

Let us be grateful that Christ saved us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us. He took on himself all the punishment our sins deserve, so we don’t have to suffer forever. Sinners should run to this refuge and salvation. Believers can find joy in it and serve their forgiving God with happiness, because of all the spiritual gifts he gives them.

29.o: Both former mercies, and fresh mercies, should be thought on by us as motives to obedience. The hearing ear, and seeing eye, and the understanding heart, are the gift of God. All that have them, have them from him. God gives not only food and raiment, but wealth and large possessions, to many to whom he does not give grace. Many enjoy the gifts, who have not hearts to perceive the Giver, nor the true design and use of the gifts. We are bound, in gratitude and interest, as well as in duty and faithfulness, to keep the words of the covenant.
29.m:
We should think about both past kindnesses and new ones to motivate us to obey. Hearing, seeing, and understanding are all gifts from God. Anyone who has these abilities got them from God. 

God gives some people money and lots of things, but not everyone gets the grace to appreciate those gifts. Many people have the gifts but don’t see the giver or know what the gifts are really for.

Out of thanks and self-interest, we need to keep the promises of our covenant.

29.o: The national covenant made with Israel, not only typified the covenant of grace made with true believers, but also represented the outward dispensation of the gospel. Those who have been enabled to consent to the Lord's new covenant of mercy and grace in Jesus Christ, and to give up themselves to be his people, should embrace every opportunity of renewing their open profession of relation to him, and their obligation to him, as the God of salvation, walking according thereto. The sinner is described as one whose heart turns away from his God; there the mischief begins, in the evil heart of unbelief, which inclines men to depart from the living God to dead idols. Even to this sin men are now tempted, when drawn aside by their own lusts and fancies. Such men are roots that bear gall and wormwood. They are weeds which, if let alone, overspread the whole field. Satan may for a time disguise this bitter morsel, so that thou shalt not have the natural taste of it, but at the last day, if not before, the true taste shall be discerned. Notice the sinner's security in sin. Though he hears the words of the curse, yet even then he thinks himself safe from the wrath of God. There is scarcely a threatening in all the book of God more dreadful than this. Oh that presumptuous sinners would read it, and tremble! for it is a real declaration of the wrath of God, against ungodliness and unrighteousness of man.
29.m:
The agreement God made with Israel showed what the promise of forgiveness through faith in Jesus means. It also shows how the message of the gospel spreads.

People who agree to follow Jesus’s offer of mercy and become part of his family should take every chance to show openly that they belong to him. They should live in ways that honor him as the God who saves them.

Sin starts when someone’s heart moves away from God. This happens because of doubt and disbelief, which leads people to leave the living God and worship false gods instead. People today face similar temptations, often led astray by their desires.

These kinds of people are like poisonous plants that spread quickly if left unchecked. Satan might hide the bitterness of these sins for a while, making them seem harmless. But eventually, the true nature will be revealed—either here on earth or certainly in the end times.

Notice how some sinners feel secure even after hearing warnings about punishment. They think they won’t face God’s anger. One of the most frightening threats in the Bible warns against such arrogance. May those who take sin lightly read this warning and be afraid, for it truly describes God’s anger toward wrongdoing.

29.o: Idolatry would be the ruin of their nation. It is no new thing for God to bring desolating judgments on a people near to him in profession. He never does this without good reason. It concerns us to seek for the reason, that we may give glory to God, and take warning to ourselves. Thus the law of Moses leaves sinners under the curse, and rooted out of the Lord's land; but the grace of Christ toward penitent, believing sinners, plants them again in their land; and they shall no more be pulled up, being kept by the power of God.
29.m:
Idolatry would destroy their nation. It’s not unusual for God to punish those who claim to follow him closely. He only does this for good reasons. We should look for these reasons so we can honor God and learn from what happens. 

The laws given by Moses leave sinners under God’s curse and banished from his land. But through Jesus’ grace, those who truly repent and believe are welcomed back. They will not be punished again because God keeps them safe.

29.o: Moses ends his prophecy of the Jews' rejection, just as St. Paul ends his discourse on the same subject, when it began to be fulfilled, Ro 11:33. We are forbidden curiously to inquire into the secret counsels of God, and to determine concerning them. But we are directed and encouraged, diligently to seek into that which God has made known. He has kept back nothing that is profitable for us, but only that of which it is good for us to be ignorant. The end of all Divine revelation is, not to furnish curious subjects of speculation and discourse, but that we may do all the words of this law, and be blessed in our deed. This, the Bible plainly reveals; further than this, man cannot profitably go. By this light he may live and die comfortably, and be happy for ever.
29.m:
Moses ends his prophecy about the Jews being rejected, just as St. Paul does when talking about the same thing, right before it starts happening, Romans 11:33. 

We shouldn't try to figure out God’s hidden plans. Instead, we should look closely at what God has revealed to us. He hasn’t kept anything from us that would help us grow, except things it’s better for us not to know.

The purpose of God’s messages isn’t to give us topics for deep thinking and discussion. It’s so we can follow all the commands in the law and be blessed by doing them. The Bible clearly tells us this much. Beyond that, there’s no benefit for us to explore more deeply.

With this guidance, people can live and die happily and find true happiness forever.

30.o: In this chapter is a plain intimation of the mercy God has in store for Israel in the latter days. This passage refers to the prophetic warnings of the last two chapters, which have been mainly fulfilled in the destruction of Jerusalem by the Romans, and in their dispersion to the present day; and there can be no doubt that the prophetic promise contained in these verses yet remain to come to pass. The Jewish nation shall in some future period, perhaps not very distant, be converted to the faith of Christ; and, many think, again settled in the land of Canaan. The language here used is in a great measure absolute promises; not merely a conditional engagement, but declaring an event assuredly to take place. For the Lord himself here engages to “circumcise their hearts;” and when regenerating grace has removed corrupt nature, and Divine love has supplanted the love of sin, they certainly will reflect, repent, return to God, and obey him; and he will rejoice in doing them good. The change that will be wrought upon them will not be only outward, or consisting in mere opinions; it will reach to their souls. It will produce in them an utter hatred of all sin, and a fervent love to God, as their reconciled God in Christ Jesus; they will love him with all their hearts, and with all their soul. They are very far from this state of mind at present, but so were the murderers of the Lord Jesus, on the day of Pentecost; who yet in one hour were converted unto God. So shall it be in the day of God's power; a nation shall be born in a day; the Lord will hasten it in his time. As a conditional promise this passage belongs to all persons and all people, not to Israel only; it assures us that the greatest sinners, if they repent and are converted, shall have their sins pardoned, and be restored to God's favour.
30.m:
This chapter shows the kindness God plans to show Israel in the future. The earlier parts talk about events that happened when Jerusalem was destroyed by the Romans and Jews were scattered. But there’s more to come. Someday soon, the Jewish people might turn back to Christianity. Some believe they’ll even live in the land of Canaan again. 

Here, God makes strong promises, not just conditions. He says he will “cut away their hearts,” removing bad thoughts and replacing them with love for God. When this happens, they will surely see their mistakes, feel sorry, and follow God. God will be happy to bless them then. 

The changes won’t just be on the surface. They’ll go deep inside, making them hate sin and love God deeply. Right now, the Jewish people don't act this way, but on the Day of Pentecost, those who killed Jesus changed quickly. In God’s time, a whole nation could change fast too. 

These promises aren’t just for Israel. All people, including big sinners, can get forgiveness and God’s favor if they turn to him.

30.o: The law is not too high for thee. It is not only known afar off; it is not confined to men of learning. It is written in thy books, made plain, so that he who runs may read it. It is in thy mouth, in the tongue commonly used by thee, in which thou mayest hear it read, and talk of it among thy children. It is delivered so that it is level to the understanding of the meanest. This is especially true of the gospel of Christ, to which the apostle applies it. But the word is nigh us, and Christ in that word; so that if we believe with the heart, that the promises of the Messiah are fulfilled in our Lord Jesus, and confess them with our mouth, we then have Christ with us.
30.m:
The law isn't too hard for you to understand. It's not just for people who are very smart. 

It’s written in your books where anyone can see it clearly. You can find it in the language you speak, so you can listen to it being read and talk about it with your kids.

It’s explained simply enough for everyone to grasp, even those who aren’t very bright. 

This is especially true for the good news about Christ. The apostle said it applies here too. 

But God’s message is close to us, and Christ is in that message. So if you believe in your heart that what was promised about the Messiah has come true through Jesus, and you tell others about it, then Christ is with you.

30.o: What could be said more moving, and more likely to make deep and lasting impressions? Every man wishes to obtain life and good, and to escape death and evil; he desires happiness, and dreads misery. So great is the compassion of the Lord, that he has favoured men, by his word, with such a knowledge of good and evil as will make them for ever happy, if it be not their own fault. Let us hear the sum of the whole matter. If they and theirs would love God, and serve him, they should live and be happy. If they or theirs should turn from God, desert his service, and worship other gods, that would certainly be their ruin. There never was, since the fall of man, more than one way to heaven; which is marked out in both Testaments, though not with equal clearness. Moses meant that same way of acceptance, which Paul more plainly described; and Paul's words mean the same obedience, on which Moses more fully treated. In both Testaments the good and right way is brought near, and plainly revealed to us.
30.m:
What could be more powerful and meaningful? Everyone wants to have a good life and avoid bad things. People want to be happy and not suffer. The Lord’s mercy is so great that he gave people enough understanding through his word to help them find true happiness forever, unless they choose otherwise. 

Let me explain the main points. If you and those around you love God and follow him, then you will live happily. But if you or anyone else turns away from God and worships other gods, that will lead to destruction. Since the time of Adam’s sin, there has only been one path to heaven. This path is shown clearly in both the Old and New Testaments, even if the details differ slightly. What Moses talked about matches what Paul explained later. Both were talking about the same way to please God. In both parts of the Bible, the right path is made clear and close to us.

31.o: Moses assures Israel of the constant presence of God with them. This is applied by the apostle to all God's spiritual Israel, to encourage their faith and hope; unto us is this gospel preached, as well as unto them; he will never fail thee, nor forsake thee, Heb 13:5. Moses commends Joshua to them for a leader; one whose wisdom, and courage, and affection they had long known; one whom God had appointed to be their leader; and therefore would own and bless. Joshua is well pleased to be admonished by Moses to be strong and of good courage. Those shall speed well, who have God with them; therefore they ought to be of good courage. Through God let us do valiantly, for through him we shall do victoriously; if we resist the devil, he will flee from us.
31.m:
Moses tells Israel that God will always be with them. The apostle uses this promise to encourage all of God’s people. He says God will never leave us or abandon us, just like He promised in Hebrews 13:5. 

Moses recommends Joshua as their new leader. They know Joshua because he has been wise, brave, and caring for a long time. God chose Joshua to lead them, so they should trust that God will support him. 

Joshua feels happy about being reminded by Moses to stay strong and confident. When God is on our side, we can feel brave too. We should fight bravely because God helps us win. If we stand up to the devil, he will run away.

31.o: Though we read the word in private, we must not think it needless to hear it read in public. This solemn reading of the law must be done in the year of release. The year of release was typical of gospel grace, which is called the acceptable year of the Lord; for our pardon and liberty by Christ, engage us to keep his commandments. It must be read to all Israel, men, women, children, and to the strangers. It is the will of God that all people should acquaint themselves with his word. It is a rule to all, therefore should be read to all. Whoever has read of the pains taken by many persons to get scraps of the Scriptures, when a whole copy could not be obtained, or safely possessed, will see how thankful we should be for the thousands of copies amongst us. They will also understand the very different situation in which the Israelites were placed for many ages. But the heart of man is so careless, that all will be found too little, to keep up a knowledge of the truths, precepts, and worship of God.
31.m:
Even if you read the Bible on your own, you still need to listen to it being read aloud in church. 

The law had to be read during the year when debts were forgiven. That special year pointed to Jesus’ forgiveness through the gospel. When God forgives us, he wants us to follow his rules.

Everyone should hear the law—men, women, children, even foreigners. God wants everyone to know his word. Since it’s important for all, it needs to be shared with all.

Many people risked their lives just to have small parts of the Bible. Today, we’re lucky to have so many Bibles around us. We should be grateful for that.

People back then didn’t always pay enough attention to God’s teachings. Even now, we might not focus enough on what God tells us.

31.o: Moses and Joshua attended the Divine Majesty at the door of the tabernacle. Moses is told again that he must shortly die; even those who are most ready and willing to die, need to be often reminded of its coming. The Lord tells Moses, that, after his death, the covenant he had taken so much pains to make between Israel and their God, would certainly be broken. Israel would forsake Him; then God would forsake Israel. Justly does he cast those off who so unjustly cast him off. Moses is directed to deliver them a song, which should remain a standing testimony for God, as faithful to them in giving them warning, and against them, as persons false to themselves in not taking the warning. The word of God is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of men's hearts, and meets them by reproofs and correction. Ministers who preach the word, know not the imaginations of men; but God, whose word it is, knows perfectly.
31.m:
Moses and Joshua went to speak with God at the entrance of the tabernacle. God reminds Moses that he will soon die, even though Moses was eager to go. 

God tells Moses that after he dies, the people of Israel will break the agreement he worked hard to establish between them and God. They will turn away from God, and God will turn away from them. It’s only fair that God leaves those who leave him first.

Moses is asked to give the people a song. This song will stay forever as proof that God faithfully warned them, while showing how they were unfaithful to themselves by ignoring the warnings.

The words of God show what people really think and feel inside. These words correct and warn us. Preachers don’t know what people are thinking, but God, who gave these words, understands everything about us.

31.o: The solemn delivery of the book of the law to the Levites, to be deposited in, or rather by the side, of the ark, is again related. The song which follows in the next chapter is delivered to Moses, and by him to the people. He wrote it first, as the Holy Spirit taught him; and then spake it in the hearing of all the people. Moses tells them plainly, I know that after my death ye will utterly corrupt yourselves. Many a sad thought, no doubt, it occasioned to this good man; but his comfort was, that he had done his duty, and that God would be glorified in their dispersion, if not in their settlement, for the foundation of God stands sure.
31.m:
The serious handing over of the book of the law to the Levites, who were to keep it near the ark, is described again. 

Moses gave the song that comes in the next chapter to the people. First, he wrote it down as the Holy Spirit guided him. Then he spoke it out loud so everyone could hear.

Moses told them clearly, “I know that after I die, you will completely turn away from what is right.” This must have caused many sad thoughts for this good man. But he found comfort knowing he had done his duty. And he trusted that even if God’s people were scattered, God would still be honored, because God’s plan remains solid.

32.o: Moses begins with a solemn appeal to heaven and earth, concerning the truth and importance of what he was about to say. His doctrine is the gospel, the speech of God, the doctrine of Christ; the doctrine of grace and mercy through him, and of life and salvation by him.
32.m: Moses starts by asking heaven and earth to listen. He wants them to know how important what he is going to say really is. What he teaches is the good news from God. It’s the message about Jesus. It tells us we can receive grace and mercy through him. And it talks about finding life and salvation because of him.

32.o: “He is a Rock.” This is the first time God is called so in Scripture. The expression denotes that the Divine power, faithfulness, and love, as revealed in Christ and the gospel, form a foundation which cannot be changed or moved, on which we may build our hopes of happiness. And under his protection we may find refuge from all our enemies, and in all our troubles; as the rocks in those countries sheltered from the burning rays of the sun, and from tempests, or were fortresses from the enemy. “His work is perfect:” that of redemption and salvation, in which there is a display of all the Divine perfection, complete in all its parts. All God's dealings with his creatures are regulated by wisdom which cannot err, and perfect justice. He is indeed just and right; he takes care that none shall lose by him. A high charge is exhibited against Israel. Even God's children have their spots, while in this imperfect state; for if we say we have no sin, no spot, we deceive ourselves. But the sin of Israel was not habitual, notorious, unrepented sin; which is a certain mark of the children of Satan. They were fools to forsake their mercies for lying vanities. All wilful sinners, especially sinners in Israel, are unwise and ungrateful.
32.m:
"He is a Rock." This is the first time God is called that in the Bible. It means that the power, faithfulness, and love shown through Jesus and the good news provide an unchanging foundation where we can place our hope for happiness. We can also seek safety from all trouble and enemies under His protection, just like people hide behind rocks during storms or heat.

“His work is perfect.” When God redeems us and saves us, He shows all His goodness without flaw. Everything He does with us follows wise rules that never go wrong. He acts fairly too, making sure no one loses out. 

There’s a serious accusation made against Israel here. Even God’s children sometimes do wrong things when they’re still learning. If someone says they’ve done nothing bad, they’re fooling themselves. But Israel wasn’t doing constant, obvious sins that show they belong to evil. Still, they acted foolishly by leaving God’s kindness for useless idols. Anyone who chooses to sin, especially those in Israel, is being both stupid and unthankful.

32.o: Moses gives particular instances of God's kindness and concern for them. The eagle's care for her young is a beautiful emblem of Christ's love, who came between Divine justice and our guilty souls, and bare our sins in his own body on the tree. And by the preached gospel, and the influences of the Holy Spirit, He stirs up and prevails upon sinners to leave Satan's bondage. In ver. #(13, 14), are emblems of the conquest believers have over their spiritual enemies, sin, Satan, and the world, in and through Christ. Also of their safety and triumph in him; of their happy frames of soul, when they are above the world, and the things of it. This will be the blessed case of spiritual Israel in every sense in the latter day.
32.m:
Moses talks about examples of God’s kindness toward people. An eagle taking care of its babies shows how much Jesus loves us. He stepped in between God’s anger and our sins, carrying those sins on the cross. Through the preaching of the gospel and the power of the Holy Spirit, he helps sinners break free from Satan’s control.

In verses 13 and 14, we see symbols of how believers defeat their enemies—sin, Satan, and the world—with help from Christ. These verses also show how safe and victorious believers are because of Christ. They describe times when believers rise above worldly concerns. 

This will be true especially for spiritual Israel in the future.

32.o: Here are two instances of the wickedness of Israel, each was apostacy from God. These people were called Jeshurun, “an upright people,” so some; “a seeing people,” so others: but they soon lost the reputation both of their knowledge and of their righteousness. They indulged their appetites, as if they had nothing to do but to make provision for the flesh to fulfil the lusts of it. Those who make a god of themselves, and a god of their bellies, in pride and wantonness, and cannot bear to be told of it, thereby forsake God, and show they esteem him lightly. There is but one way of a sinner's acceptance and sanctification, however different modes of irreligion, or false religion, may show that favourable regard for other ways, which is often miscalled candid. How mad are idolaters, who forsake the Rock of salvation, to run themselves upon the rock of perdition!
32.m:
Here are two examples of how bad Israel became by leaving God. Some people called them Jeshurun, which means either an upright people or a wise people. But they quickly lost their good name.

They focused on satisfying their desires, thinking all they needed to do was feed their bodies and satisfy their cravings. People who put themselves above everything else, including God, and act proud and wild, are really turning away from God. When someone makes their stomach or their selfish desires more important than God, they are showing that they don’t think much of God anymore.

There’s only one true path for sinners to find forgiveness and become holy. Even though there might seem to be many paths, only one leads to God’s favor. Idol worshippers are very foolish because they leave the safe Rock of salvation to run toward destruction instead.

32.o: The revolt of Israel was described in the foregoing verses, and here follow the resolves of Divine justice as to them. We deceive ourselves, if we think that God will be mocked by a faithless people. Sin makes us hateful in the sight of the holy God. See what mischief sin does, and reckon those to be fools that mock at it.
32.m: The rebellion of Israel is described in the earlier parts, and now we see God’s plans for dealing with them. We fool ourselves if we believe God will ignore the tricks of unfaithful people. Sin makes us disgusting to the holy God. Look at all the harm sin causes, and consider those who laugh at it to be foolish.

32.o: The idolatry and rebellions of Israel deserved, and the justice of God seemed to demand, that they should be rooted out. But He spared Israel, and continues them still to be living witnesses of the truth of the Bible, and to silence unbelievers. They are preserved for wise and holy purposes and the prophecies give us some idea what those purposes are. The Lord will never disgrace the throne of his glory. It is great wisdom, and will help much to the return of sinners to God, seriously to consider their latter end, or the future state. It is here meant particularly of what God foretold by Moses, about this people in the latter days; but it may be applied generally. Oh that men would consider the happiness they will lose, and the misery they will certainly plunge into, if they go on in their trespasses! What will be in the end thereof? Jer 5:31. For the Lord will in due time bring down the enemies of the church, in displeasure against their wickedness. When sinners deem themselves most secure, they suddenly fall into destruction. And God's time to appear for the deliverance of his people, is when things are at the worst with them. But those who trust to any rock but God, will find it fail them when they most need it. The rejection of the Messiah by the Jewish nation, is the continuance of their ancient idolatry, apostacy, and rebellion. They shall be brought to humble themselves before the Lord, to repent of their sins, and to trust in their long-rejected Mediator for salvation. Then he will deliver them, and make their prosperity great.
32.m:
The worship of idols and rebellious acts by Israel deserved punishment from God. Yet, God chose to spare them and keep them alive as proof that the Bible is true. This helps quiet those who doubt the word of God. 

God has reasons for keeping Israel around. Prophecies tell us some of these reasons. God’s honor won’t let him shame himself. Thinking about our final fate—what happens after we die—is very wise. It helps sinners turn back to God.

This especially means thinking about what God said through Moses about Israel in later times. We can apply this lesson more broadly too. People should think about the joy they’ll miss and the pain they’ll face if they keep sinning. What will happen in the end?

As the prophet Jeremiah said, “What will become of them?” God will punish the enemies of his church one day because of their evil deeds. Sinners often get caught off guard by disaster when they feel safest. 

When things look bleakest for God’s people, that’s when he steps in to save them. Relying on anything other than God will let you down when you need help most. Rejecting Jesus, the Messiah, shows that the Jews continue to worship false gods, leave God’s side, and rebel.

In the end, they will learn to humble themselves before God, admit their wrongs, and put faith in Jesus, whom they once rejected. Only then will God rescue them and bless them greatly.

32.o: This conclusion of the song speaks, 1. Glory to God. No escape can be made from his power. 2. It speaks terror to his enemies. Terror indeed to those who hate him. The wrath of God is here revealed from heaven against them. 3. It speaks comfort to his own people. The song concludes with words of joy. Whatever judgments are brought upon sinners, it shall go well with the people of God.
32.m:
This part of the song talks about three things:

1. Glory to God. There’s no way to avoid his power.
2. Fear for his enemies. Those who hate God will really be scared because his anger comes down on them from heaven.
3. Comfort for his followers. The song ends happily. Even if God punishes sinners, everything will be good for his people.
